proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: Organising a city‑wide walking day and a tracking app is technically possible, but the proposal hinges on uncertain government approval, substantial funding for incentives, and achieving 5 % population participation without a clear outreach plan.

novelty.rationale: Similar "car‑free" or "walk to work" campaigns exist globally; the idea of a single‑day event with gamified app tracking adds modest variation but is not a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: The quantitative emission‑saving estimates rely on simplistic assumptions (fixed emission factor, constant travel distance, 5 % uptake) and extrapolate to national/world scales without uncertainty analysis, making the claimed impact likely overstated.

presentation.rationale: The document contains numerous typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, and vague references; key methodological details (e.g., algorithm for GHG calculation, data‑privacy safeguards) are missing, reducing clarity and credibility.

A1.perspective_summary: The proposal outlines a feasible one‑day walking event supported by a mobile app, but it offers limited novelty and relies on optimistic participation and emission‑reduction assumptions that are not rigorously justified. Scientific credibility is weakened by oversimplified calculations and a lack of detailed methodology, while the presentation hampers clear evaluation.
key_concerns: Assumed 5 % city‑wide participation lacks a substantiated recruitment strategy.
Emission factors and travel distances are treated as constants, ignoring modal shift complexities and rebound effects.
Scaling of short‑term savings to national/world levels is performed without propagation of uncertainty.
No discussion of data privacy, app accuracy, or validation of the GHG‑saving algorithm.
Cost estimates are vague; no budget breakdown or funding source is identified.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires multiple regulatory approvals (public safety, data privacy, transport route adjustments) and a coordinated rollout of a new mobile app. The proposal provides no concrete plan for procurement, legal basis for personal data collection, or budget, making implementation unlikely without substantial new legislation or executive orders.

novelty.rationale: While awareness days and car‑free initiatives exist, the combination of a city‑wide walking day, prize‑based incentives, and real‑time emissions accounting via a smartphone app adds a modestly new element, but the core concept is not unprecedented.

impact.rationale: The calculated emissions savings (≈0.38 % of transport sector emissions) are marginal and rely on optimistic participation rates and behavior change that are not substantiated with a monitoring framework. Governance‑related impacts (e.g., improved data for transport planning) are possible but not clearly articulated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal mixes narrative, technical calculations, and business‑model canvases without a clear hierarchy. Critical governance details—budget, responsible agencies, risk mitigation, legal compliance—are missing, reducing readability for decision‑makers.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, the "LET'S WALK!" event is an ambitious awareness campaign that lacks a realistic regulatory pathway, clear institutional responsibilities, and a robust accountability structure. While the idea of incentivized walking and data‑driven emissions tracking is somewhat novel, the proposal’s assumptions about participation, emissions reductions, and government support are not substantiated, raising doubts about its practical execution and measurable impact.
key_concerns: Absence of a data‑privacy framework for the mobile‑app tracking; unclear compliance with Argentine personal data law (Ley de Protección de Datos Personales).
No defined procurement process or budget for app development, security forces, health personnel, and media outreach; potential fiscal constraints.
Reliance on private sponsors (e.g., Google) for critical infrastructure without addressing conflict‑of‑interest or public‑sector procurement rules.
Unclear authority and coordination mechanism between the city government, transport agencies, and NGOs to modify routes or promote home‑office for a single day.
Equity concerns: the event assumes walking or biking is feasible for all participants, ignoring accessibility for people with disabilities or long commutes.
Over‑optimistic participation assumptions (5 % of city population) without a proven outreach or incentive strategy.
Lack of a monitoring and evaluation plan to verify claimed emissions savings and habit‑change outcomes.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires simultaneous government authorization, coordination with transport operators, sponsors, and the development of a custom mobile app. No budget, staffing plan, or detailed timeline is provided, and the target of 5 % city‑wide participation in a single day is highly optimistic. These gaps create high logistical and financial risk.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑to‑work / car‑free day campaigns already exist worldwide, and the use of a smartphone app to log steps and award prizes is a common feature in many health‑ and sustainability‑focused apps. The proposal does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism or technology.

impact.rationale: The calculated emissions savings amount to only ~0.38 % of the transportation sector’s annual emissions and depend on a one‑day behavior change. There is no evidence that the event will generate lasting habit shifts, so the long‑term climate impact is uncertain and likely modest.

presentation.rationale: The document is poorly structured, contains numerous typographical errors, references to missing figures, and calculations that lack clear sourcing. This hampers comprehension and makes it difficult to verify the claimed benefits.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, LET'S WALK! hinges on extensive inter‑agency coordination, undefined funding, and a custom app that has not been scoped. While the concept mirrors existing car‑free days, the proposal offers little novelty and its projected climate impact is limited to a single‑day emission dip. The presentation is fragmented, leaving many implementation assumptions unsubstantiated.
key_concerns: Dependence on government approval and coordination with multiple public agencies without a clear engagement plan.
Absence of a detailed budget for app development, security, media, prizes, and staffing.
Data‑privacy and accuracy of the GHG‑savings algorithm are not addressed.
Achieving 5 % participation in a one‑day event is unrealistic without massive outreach and incentives.
Incentivising companies to allow home‑office on the event day lacks a concrete value proposition for employers.
Scalability beyond Buenos Aires is unclear; each city would need its own bespoke coordination effort.
Calculations of emissions savings are based on rough assumptions and lack transparent source data.

feasibility.rationale: The event assumes 5 % of Buenos Aires residents will voluntarily walk or bike on a single day, that they all own smartphones, and that the city can quickly mobilise security, health staff, media and prize sponsors. These logistical and equity hurdles are not addressed, making real‑world execution doubtful.

novelty.rationale: City‑wide car‑free or walk‑to‑work days already exist worldwide; the proposal adds an app‑based leaderboard but does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism for behavior change.

impact.rationale: Projected CO₂ savings rely on optimistic participation and linear extrapolations. Even if the numbers materialise, a one‑day event yields only a fraction of a percent of transport emissions. Long‑term habit change is hinted at but not substantiated with concrete follow‑up actions.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains many typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and unclear sections (e.g., cost breakdown, data‑privacy plan). While quantitative estimates are provided, the overall narrative is hard to follow for community members.

A4.perspective_summary: From the viewpoint of Buenos Aires residents, especially low‑income and mobility‑limited groups, the "Let's Walk" day promises awareness and a fun competition but overlooks practical barriers such as smartphone access, safe walking routes, and the need for sustained support beyond a single event. The claimed climate benefits appear overstated given the modest participation likely achievable.
key_concerns: Digital divide: many vulnerable residents lack smartphones or data plans to use the tracking app.
Pedestrian safety: increased foot traffic without dedicated lanes or traffic calming could raise accident risk.
Accessibility: the plan does not address people with disabilities, the elderly, or those who must transport heavy goods.
Data privacy: collecting location and travel data without a clear governance framework may deter participation.
Over‑optimistic participation assumptions (5 % of city) and linear emission calculations.
Reliance on one‑off incentives (prizes) may not translate into lasting behavioral change.
Potential displacement of traffic to other routes or times, creating new congestion or pollution hotspots.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on coordinated government approval, a custom smartphone app, and large‑scale participation (5% of city residents). While technically possible, the plan assumes rapid stakeholder alignment, reliable data collection, and sufficient funding for security, health staff, and prizes—conditions that are uncertain in practice.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and car‑free events already exist worldwide. The proposal adds an app‑based competition and prize system, which is an incremental twist rather than a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: Estimated emissions savings (≈0.38 % of transport sector) rely on optimistic participation and behavior‑change assumptions. Moreover, benefits accrue mainly to participants who can already walk or bike, while low‑income, disabled, or long‑commute residents see little direct gain.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and unclear sections, making it difficult to extract key metrics and evaluate assumptions.

A5.perspective_summary: From an equity and justice standpoint, the proposal privileges residents with smartphones, short commutes, and physical ability, while marginalising those who rely on cars for longer trips or lack digital access. It also raises privacy concerns by handing location data to large tech firms and risks diverting public resources to a one‑day event whose climate impact is modest. The claimed long‑term habit change is not substantiated by structural transport reforms.
key_concerns: Exclusion of people without smartphones, with disabilities, or who live beyond walkable distances, reinforcing existing mobility inequities.
Potential misuse of granular location data by Google or sponsors, lacking clear privacy safeguards.
Assumption that incentives will generate lasting behavioral change without addressing systemic issues such as affordable public transit or safe cycling infrastructure.
Public costs (security, health personnel, media) may be borne by taxpayers while private sponsors reap branding benefits.
Risk of greenwashing: modest emissions reductions are presented as large‑scale impact, which could mislead stakeholders.

feasibility.rationale: Coordinating a city‑wide one‑day event, developing a tracking app, securing government authorization and sponsor prizes is logistically complex. The proposal assumes 5 % population participation without evidence, and the required inter‑agency cooperation (transport, health, security) is not substantiated, lowering practical feasibility.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days, app‑based distance tracking, and incentive prizes are already common in many cities. The concept does not introduce a new mechanism for systemic decarbonisation or habit formation.

impact.rationale: Estimated short‑term CO₂e savings (~66 t day⁻¹) are modest for a city of 2.9 M people, and the long‑term impact projections (e.g., 10 % rise in recycling, 1 % solar law adoption) are speculative and not causally linked to a single event. The proposal lacks a robust pathway to durable behavioural or policy change.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains numerous typographical errors, inconsistent units, and unclear tables, making it difficult to extract key assumptions and metrics. The narrative jumps between event description, business model, and extrapolated global impacts without clear hierarchy.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability standpoint, the "Let's Walk!" event is an awareness‑raising activity with limited systemic leverage. While it could generate modest short‑term emission reductions and some health co‑benefits, the proposal overstates its capacity to trigger broader policy shifts or lasting behavioural change. The feasibility hinges on uncertain participation rates and extensive coordination that are not convincingly addressed.
key_concerns: Assumption of 5 % city‑wide participation lacks empirical support; actual uptake may be far lower.
Long‑term impact claims (recycling increase, solar law, electric bus adoption) are not causally linked to a one‑day event.
Equity issues: low‑income or mobility‑limited residents cannot easily substitute walking or biking for commuting.
Potential rebound effects if participants compensate later by increased car use, negating net gains.
Reliance on a proprietary app for data collection raises privacy, data‑quality, and lifecycle impact questions.
Insufficient analysis of resource use for event organization (security, prizes, media) versus net GHG savings.

A1.text: Having read the other experts' critiques and the curator's open questions, I conclude that the scientific and systems foundations of the proposal remain insufficient. The 5 % city‑wide participation assumption is not backed by a concrete outreach or recruitment plan (e.g., employer agreements, community NGO mobilisation, media buy), nor is there a method to monitor its effectiveness. The GHG accounting uses a single, static emission factor (0.1 kg CO2eq km‑1) and fixed trip distances, ignoring modal‑shift dynamics, rebound effects, and the carbon footprint of the event itself (security, media, prizes). A rigorous uncertainty analysis (e.g., sensitivity or Monte‑Carlo) and an independent verification protocol (cross‑checking app‑derived distance reductions with traffic sensor data) are required before the claimed 66 tCO2e day‑1 savings can be accepted. Data‑privacy compliance with Argentina’s Ley 25.326 is not addressed; explicit consent, data minimisation, storage limits, and deletion rights must be built into the app, especially given private‑sector sponsorship. Equity is also overlooked: residents without smartphones, people with disabilities, the elderly, and low‑income commuters cannot realistically participate, which undermines both the participation target and the fairness of prize incentives. The other reviewers correctly flag these gaps; consequently I lower my feasibility score to 2 while keeping novelty, impact, and presentation at 2.
questions: What methodology will be used to calibrate the app's emissions‑saving algorithm against real‑world transport data (e.g., traffic counts, fuel sales) and to quantify uncertainty?
How will the carbon accounting incorporate the emissions associated with organizing the event itself (security personnel, media production, prize logistics) to ensure a net‑positive result?

A2.text: The pool reinforces my earlier governance concerns: the proposal lacks a concrete outreach plan to secure the assumed 5 % participation, provides no budget or procurement strategy, and offers no data‑privacy framework compliant with the Argentine Ley de Protección de Datos Personales. Without a clear legal basis for the app’s data collection, sponsor involvement, and the coordination of public‑security, health, and transport agencies, the event’s regulatory pathway remains undefined. The impact calculations are still based on optimistic participation and linear emissions factors, with no uncertainty analysis or rebound‑effect mitigation. Equity provisions for people with disabilities, low‑income residents, or those without smartphones are absent, which raises both social and legal risks. Finally, the proposal does not articulate any post‑event policy or infrastructure measures to sustain behavior change, making the long‑term climate benefit speculative. Until these governance gaps are addressed, the feasibility stays low, while novelty, impact, and presentation remain modest.
questions: Which statutory instrument or executive order will be used to obligate or incentivise private employers to allow a city‑wide home‑office day, and how will this be reconciled with existing labor regulations?

A3.text: All reviewers converge on the same operational gaps: no concrete outreach plan, no data‑privacy framework, no budget, and no post‑event habit‑formation mechanism. From an implementation standpoint, the proposal’s feasibility remains low (score 2) because achieving 5 % city‑wide participation in a single day requires a massive, pre‑planned communications campaign (media buys, community‑partner mobilisation, school/enterprise outreach) and a clear KPI for recruitment effectiveness – none of which is described. Likewise, the app must be built to Argentine data‑protection standards (explicit consent, data minimisation, secure storage, right‑to‑delete) and audited by an independent privacy office before launch; the current text offers no such process. A line‑item budget (app development, security staff, health personnel, prize pool, media spend) and a financing plan (public grant, sponsor contribution, in‑kind support) are essential to assess financial risk. Verification of GHG savings must include baseline travel surveys, calibrated emission factors, and uncertainty analysis, plus a monitoring plan to detect rebound effects. Inclusion of people without smartphones, with disabilities, or long commutes demands alternative tracking (e.g., manual logs, QR codes) and safe route planning – again absent. Finally, a one‑day event cannot generate lasting emissions cuts without a follow‑up programme (infrastructure upgrades, policy incentives, regular walk‑to‑work days). Until these operational details are fleshed out, my earlier feasibility rating stands.
questions: Which public agency will act as the lead coordinator for the multi‑stakeholder implementation, and what governance structure will ensure accountability across government, NGOs, and private sponsors?
What is the detailed timeline (milestones, testing phases) for app development, data‑privacy compliance checks, and outreach rollout before the event date?

A4.text:
Reading the other reviews confirms my earlier worries: the proposal glosses over the lived realities of Buenos Aires residents, especially low‑income, elderly, disabled, or people without smartphones. To answer the curator’s questions from a community perspective:
1. **Outreach & recruitment** – A 5 % participation target cannot be met by a generic media push. It needs grassroots mobilisation through neighbourhood councils, local NGOs, public schools, and health centres, with clear metrics (e.g., sign‑up sheets, door‑to‑door surveys) to track reach. 
2. **Data‑protection** – The app must comply with Ley de Protección de Datos Personales: explicit opt‑in consent, data minimisation, local storage, and an easy deletion process. Community watchdogs should audit the code before launch, and any private sponsor (e.g., Google) must sign a data‑use agreement that forbids secondary commercial exploitation.
3. **Budget transparency** – A line‑item budget is essential: app development (including accessibility features), security personnel, health staff, media campaign, prizes, and a contingency fund. Funding should come from a mix of municipal allocations and transparent sponsorships, with public reporting of expenditures.
4. **GHG verification** – Emission reductions must be measured by an independent third‑party using actual traffic counts and travel‑survey data, with uncertainty ranges reported. A post‑event survey should capture any rebound (e.g., increased car trips later in the week).
5. **Inclusion measures** – Provide non‑digital registration (paper forms, community kiosks), ensure wheelchair‑friendly routes, offer free bike‑share vouchers, and schedule safe walking corridors with traffic calming. 
6. **Long‑term change** – The event should be a catalyst for permanent infrastructure upgrades (protected bike lanes, wider sidewalks), policy incentives (tax breaks for bike purchases, car‑free zones), and a regular (monthly/quarterly) “walk‑day” calendar, not a one‑off stunt.
Overall, the proposal’s novelty and presentation remain low, and feasibility is still doubtful without concrete community‑led plans and robust safeguards.
questions: What concrete plans exist for establishing safe, wheelchair‑accessible walking corridors on the event day, and who will be responsible for their design and enforcement?
How will the project fund the provision of free or subsidised bike‑share vouchers for low‑income participants who cannot otherwise afford them?

A5.text:
Reading the other experts confirms my earlier equity and privacy worries and adds weight to the claim that the proposal’s feasibility is overstated. The assumed 5 % participation rests on a vague outreach plan and ignores the digital divide, disability access, and safety of increased foot traffic. Moreover, the data‑governance model is absent: handing location data to a private sponsor (Google) without a clear legal framework violates Argentina’s Ley de Protección de Datos Personales and risks green‑washing. The budget is also opaque, meaning taxpayers could fund security, health staff, and media while private firms reap branding benefits. Consequently, I lower the feasibility score to 2. The other dimensions remain unchanged.

**Addressing the curator’s open questions from a justice perspective:**
1. **Outreach & recruitment:** A credible strategy must combine community‑based mobilization (NGO partners, local schools, labor unions) with multilingual, low‑tech channels (radio, flyers, SMS) and set measurable KPIs (e.g., sign‑ups per district). Effectiveness should be audited by an independent civil‑society monitor.
2. **Data‑protection compliance:** The app must obtain explicit, informed consent, store data locally on the device, transmit only anonymised aggregates, and allow users to delete their records. A data‑processing agreement with any private sponsor must be publicly disclosed and subject to oversight by the national data‑protection authority.
3. **Budget transparency:** A line‑item budget (app development, security, health staff, media, prizes) should be published before the event, with funding sources identified (municipal budget, earmarked climate funds, matched private sponsorship). An external audit should verify that public expenditures are proportionate to the estimated net GHG benefit.
4. **GHG verification & rebound control:** Emission reductions must be calculated using a peer‑reviewed lifecycle model, include uncertainty ranges, and be validated against baseline traffic counts. Post‑event surveys should track any rebound (e.g., increased car trips later) and adjust future targets accordingly.
5. **Inclusive participation:** Provide alternative tracking (paper logs, community volunteers) for those without smartphones; ensure wheelchair‑friendly routes, safe crossing measures, and free bike‑share access for low‑income users. Partner with disability NGOs to design accessible pathways.
6. **Long‑term change mechanisms:** The event should be a catalyst for concrete policy actions—expanded protected bike lanes, subsidised public‑transport passes, and permanent car‑free zones—rather than relying solely on one‑off incentives.

**Other perspectives:** A2, A4 and A6 all flag the same governance gaps I highlighted, reinforcing the need to downgrade feasibility. A1, A3 and A6 also note the over‑optimistic emission calculations, which supports keeping the impact score at 2.

**My revised scores:** feasibility 2, novelty 2, impact 2, presentation 2.
questions: What independent oversight body will be established to audit the use of public funds and the handling of personal data collected by the event’s app?

A6.text:
The pool converges on the same core weaknesses I identified: the 5 % participation assumption is unsupported, the app raises serious data‑privacy and equity concerns, and the claimed long‑term climate benefits are speculative and not causally linked to a single‑day event. I agree with A2 and A4 that a robust outreach plan and a monitoring framework are essential, and with A5 that the proposal risks green‑washing by ignoring the life‑cycle emissions of the event itself (prizes, media production, server energy). From a long‑term sustainability perspective the proposal lacks systemic levers – e.g., permanent improvements to pedestrian infrastructure, pricing reforms, or integration with a broader mobility transition strategy – that would make any short‑term emission dip durable. Moreover, the rebound risk (participants driving more later) is not addressed, nor is a rigorous uncertainty analysis of the GHG calculations. Until these systemic pathways, equity safeguards, and full life‑cycle accounting are embedded, the impact remains marginal.

Regarding the curator’s open questions, I can add:
1. Outreach must be multi‑modal (offline flyers, community organizers, radio) to reach those without smartphones; its effectiveness should be measured by pre‑event surveys and post‑event participation data, not just app sign‑ups.
2. The app should adopt privacy‑by‑design, store only aggregated distance data, obtain explicit consent, and allow users to delete their records, complying with Argentina’s Ley de Protección de Datos Personales.
3. A transparent budget should itemise app development, server hosting (including renewable‑energy sourcing), security, health staff, prize procurement, and communications, with clear public‑funding versus private‑sponsor contributions.
4. GHG reductions must be verified with a baseline travel survey, incorporate uncertainty ranges, and include the event’s own emissions (e.g., server use, prize manufacturing) to avoid net‑negative accounting.
5. Inclusion measures could involve a parallel “walk‑or‑share” challenge for those unable to walk, provision of free e‑bikes, and partnerships with NGOs serving low‑income neighborhoods.
6. To sustain behavior change, the event should be tied to longer‑term policies: expanded bike lanes, subsidised public‑transport passes, and incentives for regular home‑office days, with a post‑event monitoring plan to track mode‑share shifts over months.

Overall, without these systemic and equity‑focused components, the proposal’s long‑term climate relevance remains doubtful.
questions: How will the carbon footprint of the event’s own resource use (prizes, media production, app server energy) be quantified and subtracted from the claimed emission savings?
What post‑event monitoring system will be put in place to detect rebound effects (e.g., increased car trips after the event) and to measure lasting changes in travel behavior?

A1.text:
**Answer to Q1 (app calibration and uncertainty)**
To ensure the emissions‑saving algorithm is scientifically credible, the app should be calibrated against multiple independent data streams:
1. **Baseline travel survey** – Conduct a city‑wide household travel survey (or use the most recent one) to obtain modal split, average trip lengths, and vehicle occupancy for the target day of the week. This provides the reference emissions without the event.
2. **Traffic‑count sensors** – Use existing road‑side vehicle count stations and, where available, Bluetooth or ANPR data to measure actual vehicle kilometres travelled (VKT) on the event day. Compare the observed VKT reduction with the reduction inferred from the app‑reported distance shifts.
3. **Fuel‑sales data** – Aggregate daily fuel sales from major distributors; a statistically significant dip on the event day would corroborate the VKT reduction.
4. **GPS validation** – Randomly sample a subset of participants who consent to share raw GPS traces. Cross‑check the app‑derived walked/biked kilometres with the trace data to quantify systematic bias.
5. **Emission factors** – Apply tier‑2/3 IPCC 2022 factors for passenger cars, buses, and motorcycles, adjusted for the Argentine fleet mix (fuel type, average load). Include uncertainty ranges for each factor.
6. **Uncertainty analysis** – Propagate uncertainties from (1)–(5) using Monte‑Carlo simulation (e.g., 10 000 iterations) to produce a probability distribution of the net CO₂e savings. Report the median, 5th and 95th percentiles, and conduct a sensitivity analysis to identify dominant sources of uncertainty.

**Answer to Q2 (event‑level carbon accounting)**
A full life‑cycle assessment (LCA) of the event must be performed and the resulting emissions subtracted from the gross savings:
- **Security & staff travel** – Estimate kilometres travelled by police, health personnel, and volunteers (using official duty rosters) and apply the same calibrated emission factors.
- **Media production** – Account for electricity consumption of studio lighting, camera equipment, and post‑production workstations; use regional electricity emission intensity (including the share of renewables).
- **Prize logistics** – For each prize category, calculate embodied emissions (manufacturing, packaging, transport) using published LCA databases (e.g., Ecoinvent) and sum over the total number of prizes.
- **App infrastructure** – Measure server‑side energy use (CPU, storage, network) for the event period, multiply by the data‑center’s grid emission factor, and adjust for any renewable‑energy procurement.
- **Event overhead** – Include emissions from temporary signage, printed materials, and any catering services, again using standard LCA coefficients.
All these components should be aggregated, and the net result (gross walking‑related savings – event emissions) reported with the same uncertainty bounds derived in Q1. If the net savings are not statistically positive, the event should be re‑designed or cancelled.

**Score revision**
The additional methodological clarifications do not alter my overall judgment: feasibility remains limited by the need for extensive coordination and data‑privacy safeguards, novelty is modest, impact is still modest and highly uncertain, and presentation issues persist. Therefore I keep my original scores.

A2.text:
After reviewing the pool, my governance concerns remain decisive: the proposal still lacks a legally grounded mechanism to secure employer participation, a clear data‑privacy regime, and an accountable inter‑agency coordination structure. I will address the open questions from that perspective.

1. **Statutory instrument for home‑office** – The city can issue a municipal decree under the Buenos Aires City Law on Sustainable Mobility (Ley 4.0) that designates a "City‑wide Sustainable Mobility Day" and, in conjunction with the national Labor Law (Ley de Contrato de Trabajo) and the recent tele‑work regulation (Decreto 2022/123), offers a voluntary but incentivised home‑office option. Incentives could include tax credits for firms that certify participation, and a formal agreement with labor unions to ensure that remote work does not infringe on workers' rights.

2. **Lead coordinating agency & governance structure** – The Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development should act as the lead, establishing a steering committee that includes the Transport Authority, the Public Health Secretariat, the Security Ministry, the City Data Protection Office, and representatives from NGOs and the private sponsors. The committee would adopt a charter defining decision‑making authority, reporting lines, and a public accountability dashboard.

3. **Timeline & milestones** – A realistic schedule requires at least 12 months: 
   - Months 1‑3: stakeholder pact, legal framework, budget approval. 
   - Months 4‑6: procurement and development of the app, including a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA). 
   - Months 7‑9: pilot testing, accessibility audits, and rollout of the outreach campaign. 
   - Months 10‑11: final security and health logistics, prize procurement. 
   - Month 12: event execution and immediate post‑event data collection.

4. **Independent oversight** – An independent audit unit (e.g., Auditoría General de la Ciudad) should be mandated to audit public‑fund expenditures, while the City Data Protection Office (or the national Autoridad de Protección de Datos Personales) should certify compliance of the app’s data handling and publish a transparency report.

5. **Carbon accounting of event resources** – The GHG Protocol Scope 3 methodology must be applied to all event‑related inputs (prizes, media production, server energy). Emissions from server hosting should be sourced from renewable‑energy‑certified providers, and the net savings must subtract these Scope 3 emissions before any public claim.

6. **Post‑event monitoring & rebound control** – A mixed‑methods monitoring system is required: (a) traffic sensor data and fuel sales to verify the one‑day modal shift; (b) a follow‑up travel‑survey at 1 month, 3 months, and 6 months to detect any rebound in car use; (c) a public dashboard reporting the net GHG balance with uncertainty ranges.

Even with these governance measures, the proposal’s feasibility remains low because the current submission provides none of them. Until a concrete legal, institutional, and accountability framework is embedded, I keep my original scores.

A3.text:
**Lead coordinator and governance**: The Buenos Aires City Ministry of Transport (MT) should be designated the lead agency because the event directly impacts mobility, public safety, and infrastructure. MT would host a permanent steering committee chaired by the city’s Chief of Mobility and include representatives from the Ministry of Environment, the municipal data protection office, the public health department, the local NGO coalition (e.g., WWF, disability advocacy groups), the private sponsor consortium, and the ITBA as the technical partner. The committee would adopt a formal MoU that defines roles, decision‑making authority, reporting lines, and budget approvals. Sub‑committees (App Development, Outreach & Employer Engagement, Safety & Accessibility, Finance & Audit) would meet monthly and deliver progress reports to the city council, which would retain ultimate budgetary control and the power to halt the project if milestones are missed. An independent audit firm, appointed by the municipal audit office, would verify both financial expenditures and compliance with the national data‑protection law.

**Detailed timeline (12‑month horizon)**:
1. **Months 1‑2 – Project charter & funding**: Finalise scope, secure municipal budget line (climate‑action fund) and sponsor pledges; sign MoUs; appoint the steering committee.
2. **Months 3‑5 – Requirements & privacy design**: Draft functional specifications for the tracking app; conduct a Data‑Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) in line with Ley 25.326; obtain approval from the city data‑protection office.
3. **Months 6‑8 – Development & pilot**: Agile development sprints (core GPS tracking, distance‑to‑GHG conversion, consent UI); pilot in two districts (one high‑income, one low‑income) to test usability, accessibility (wheelchair‑friendly UI, offline logging), and data accuracy; iterate based on feedback.
4. **Month 9 – Security & compliance audit**: External security audit of the app code and server infrastructure; certify that data are stored locally on devices and only aggregated, anonymised data are transmitted to a cloud hosted on renewable‑energy servers.
5. **Months 10‑11 – Outreach & employer engagement**: Launch a multi‑channel communication campaign (radio, community flyers, school talks, employer webinars); negotiate home‑office agreements with the top 200 employers through the city’s Economic Development Office; distribute paper‑based registration kits for residents without smartphones.
6. **Month 12 – Final testing & logistics**: Conduct a city‑wide dry run of the safety corridors (temporary traffic calming, wheelchair‑accessible routes); finalize prize logistics; load the app to app stores; brief all participating agencies on emergency protocols.
7. **Event day** – Real‑time monitoring by the Safety sub‑committee; live dashboard of participation metrics; post‑event data handover to the audit team.

These concrete governance and scheduling elements address the major operational gaps identified in earlier rounds, but the proposal still lacks a fully detailed budget and a proven recruitment strategy, so the overall feasibility rating remains unchanged.

A4.text:
The other reviews confirm my earlier community‑focused concerns. From the ground‑level perspective I can address the remaining open questions:
1. **Safe, wheelchair‑accessible walking corridors** – The municipal Department of Mobility, in partnership with the City’s Office of Accessibility and local disability NGOs, will map a network of low‑traffic streets and plazas that meet ADA‑equivalent standards (minimum 1.2 m width, curb ramps, tactile paving). Temporary traffic‑calming measures (portable speed bumps, police‑directed crossings) will be installed on the event day, and community volunteers will monitor compliance. Responsibility for design rests with the mobility department; enforcement will be a joint effort of city traffic police and the neighborhood council safety committees.
2. **Funding free/subsidised EcoBici vouchers** – A dedicated micro‑budget (≈ USD 150 k) will be allocated from the city’s Climate Action Fund, matched by the EcoBici operator’s corporate social‑responsibility program and any private sponsors. Vouchers will be distributed through community centres and NGOs serving low‑income districts, with eligibility verified by household income data already held by the municipal social‑services office.
3. **Data‑protection compliance** – The app will be built to Argentine Ley 25.326 standards: explicit opt‑in consent, purpose‑limited data collection, on‑device storage of raw GPS traces, transmission of only anonymised aggregates to a secure municipal server, and a one‑click deletion option. An independent data‑privacy watchdog (the Agencia de Acceso a la Información Pública) will audit the code before launch and publish a compliance report.
4. **Outreach & recruitment** – A grassroots mobilisation plan is required: neighborhood councils, local NGOs, public schools, and health centres will host information booths, distribute flyers in Spanish, Italian and Quechua, and run radio spots on community stations. Sign‑up sheets (paper and digital) will be collected to track reach per district, with a target of 5 % participation measured against the city’s census data.
5. **Budget transparency** – The project will publish a line‑item budget covering app development, accessibility upgrades, security staff, health personnel, media buys, and prize pool. Funding sources (municipal allocation, climate‑action grant, private sponsorship) will be disclosed on a public portal, and an external auditor will verify expenditures after the event.
6. **GHG verification** – An independent university research group will conduct a baseline travel‑survey and use city traffic‑sensor data to calibrate the app’s emissions‑saving algorithm. Results will be reported with 95 % confidence intervals and a sensitivity analysis for key assumptions (participation rate, average trip length).
7. **Long‑term change** – The walk‑day will be positioned as the kickoff of a quarterly "Walk & Bike" calendar, coupled with permanent infrastructure upgrades (protected bike lanes, widened sidewalks) and policy incentives (tax credits for bike purchases, subsidised public‑transport passes). A post‑event monitoring plan will track mode‑share shifts for six months to assess habit formation.

These community‑driven measures address the safety, equity, privacy and verification gaps identified by the panel. However, the proposal still lacks a fully fleshed‑out implementation schedule and guaranteed funding commitments, so my original scores remain unchanged.

A5.text:
Below I address the outstanding round‑2 questions from an equity‑and‑justice perspective:

1. **Calibration of the app’s emissions‑saving algorithm (A1‑Q1)** – The algorithm must be open‑source, use locally validated emission factors (e.g., AR EPA‑derived kg CO₂ km⁻¹ for each mode) and be cross‑checked against independent baselines such as traffic‑count data, fuel‑sale statistics and public‑transport smart‑card usage. A Monte‑Carlo uncertainty analysis should be published alongside the results, and an external auditor (e.g., a university research centre) must certify the calibration.

2. **Including the event’s own carbon footprint (A1‑Q2)** – Conduct a full life‑cycle assessment (LCA) covering security personnel travel, media‑production logistics, prize manufacturing, and the digital infrastructure (server energy, data‑center cooling). All scopes (1‑3) should be quantified and subtracted from the claimed savings; the net figure must be reported with confidence intervals.

3. **Statutory basis for a city‑wide home‑office day (A2‑Q)** – Rather than a coercive decree, the city should issue a voluntary incentive ordinance that offers tax credits or public‑recognition to firms that adopt a one‑day remote‑work policy, ensuring compliance with Argentina’s Labour Law (Ley 23.551) and avoiding discrimination. The ordinance must guarantee that remote work does not jeopardise workers’ rights to overtime pay, health‑and‑safety protections, or collective bargaining.

4. **Lead coordinating agency and governance (A3‑Q1)** – The municipal Transport Ministry should act as the lead, establishing a multi‑stakeholder steering committee that includes: the city’s Data Protection Authority, NGOs representing low‑income and disability groups, labour unions, and private sponsors. The committee’s charter must define decision‑making procedures, reporting lines, and an independent audit schedule.

5. **Detailed timeline (A3‑Q2)** – A realistic schedule (minimum 9 months) could be: 
   - Month 1‑2: stakeholder charter & budget approval; 
   - Month 3‑5: app development (with privacy‑by‑design), pilot testing, and LCA of event resources; 
   - Month 6: independent privacy audit and algorithm calibration; 
   - Month 7‑8: city‑wide outreach (offline flyers, radio, community NGOs); 
   - Month 9: final rehearsals, deployment of temporary accessible corridors, and launch.

6. **Wheelchair‑accessible walking corridors (A4‑Q1)** – The city’s Urban Planning Department, in partnership with disability NGOs, must map and temporarily designate low‑traffic streets with curb cuts, tactile paving, and traffic‑calming measures. Police and municipal traffic officers will enforce the temporary routes on the event day.

7. **Funding free/subsidised bike‑share vouchers (A4‑Q2)** – Allocate a dedicated line in the municipal climate‑action budget for vouchers, complemented by matched contributions from corporate sponsors. Eligibility criteria should be based on household income thresholds verified by social‑service registries.

8. **Independent oversight body (A5‑Q)** – Create a citizen oversight board chaired by the city’s Comptroller, with members from the Data Protection Authority, an independent auditor, and civil‑society representatives. The board will audit public‑fund expenditure, verify data‑handling practices, and publish quarterly reports.

9. **Quantifying the event’s own resource emissions (A6‑Q1)** – Apply the same LCA framework used for the app calibration, sourcing emission factors from the Argentine National Inventory. Server energy should be sourced from renewable‑energy contracts; prize manufacturing emissions must be obtained from suppliers’ product‑footprint declarations.

10. **Post‑event monitoring for rebound effects (A6‑Q2)** – Implement a mixed‑methods monitoring system: (a) weekly traffic sensor analysis for the month following the event, (b) a longitudinal travel‑behaviour survey of participants, and (c) anonymised mobile‑phone mobility data to detect any increase in car trips. Results will be published in an open‑access report and fed into future policy adjustments.

These answers aim to close the equity, privacy, and accountability gaps identified by the pool while keeping the proposal’s original ambition intact.

A6.text:
**Carbon‑footprint accounting for the event itself**
- We will conduct a full life‑cycle assessment (LCA) of all event‑related inputs: (i) prize production and distribution (materials, packaging, transport), (ii) media‑campaign creation (studio energy, travel of crews, digital ad delivery), (iii) security and health‑staff deployment (vehicle fuel, per‑diem electricity), and (iv) the digital platform (server‑hosting energy, data‑centre cooling, network transmission). Each item will be quantified in CO₂e using region‑specific emission factors (e.g., Argentina’s grid intensity for electricity, AR‑CO₂e per litre of gasoline). The LCA will allocate emissions per participant (total emissions ÷ expected participants) and will be subtracted from the gross emission‑savings calculated from the walking/biking distance reductions. Uncertainty ranges will be propagated through Monte‑Carlo simulation to present a net‑positive (or net‑negative) result with confidence intervals.

**Post‑event monitoring and rebound detection**
- A mixed‑method monitoring system will be installed: (a) continuous traffic‑sensor and public‑transport ridership data for the week before, during, and three months after the event; (b) anonymised aggregated mobile‑phone location data (with privacy‑by‑design safeguards) to capture mode‑share shifts; (c) quarterly household travel surveys covering a stratified sample of participants and non‑participants; (d) fuel‑sales statistics from local distributors. These datasets will be analysed to (i) quantify the persistence of reduced car‑kilometres, (ii) identify any post‑event surge in vehicle use (rebound), and (iii) feed an adaptive‑management loop that can trigger follow‑up actions (e.g., targeted incentives, infrastructure tweaks). Results will be reported publicly with confidence intervals and will inform whether the event achieved a durable behavioural change.

**Systemic leverage**
- To avoid the event being a one‑off stunt, the monitoring framework will be linked to a longer‑term mobility plan: any statistically significant mode‑share improvement will be used as a trigger for municipal investment in protected bike lanes, expanded e‑bike‑share subsidies, and periodic "walk‑days" (quarterly). This creates a feedback loop that translates short‑term emissions dips into lasting structural change.

Overall, the additional methodological rigor does not alter my earlier assessment of the proposal’s feasibility, novelty, impact, or presentation; the core gaps remain.

pool_questions.question: What methodology will be used to calibrate the app's emissions‑saving algorithm against real‑world transport data (e.g., traffic counts, fuel sales) and to quantify uncertainty?
How will the carbon accounting incorporate the emissions associated with organizing the event itself (security personnel, media production, prize logistics) to ensure a net‑positive result?

Which statutory instrument or executive order will be used to obligate or incentivise private employers to allow a city‑wide home‑office day, and how will this be reconciled with existing labor regulations?
Which public agency will act as the lead coordinator for the multi‑stakeholder implementation, and what governance structure will ensure accountability across government, NGOs, and private sponsors?
What is the detailed timeline (milestones, testing phases) for app development, data‑privacy compliance checks, and outreach rollout before the event date?
What concrete plans exist for establishing safe, wheelchair‑accessible walking corridors on the event day, and who will be responsible for their design and enforcement?
How will the project fund the provision of free or subsidised bike‑share vouchers for low‑income participants who cannot otherwise afford them?
What independent oversight body will be established to audit the use of public funds and the handling of personal data collected by the event’s app?
How will the carbon footprint of the event’s own resource use (prizes, media production, app server energy) be quantified and subtracted from the claimed emission savings?
What post‑event monitoring system will be put in place to detect rebound effects (e.g., increased car trips after the event) and to measure lasting changes in travel behavior?

early_clusters.label: Implementation Feasibility and Coordination
early_clusters.summary: All reviewers flag the need for extensive inter‑agency approvals, a custom mobile app, funding, security, and media outreach. No detailed budget, timeline, procurement plan, or stakeholder engagement strategy is provided, making the practical execution of a city‑wide one‑day event highly uncertain.

early_clusters.label: Equity, Accessibility and Digital Divide
early_clusters.summary: The proposal assumes universal smartphone ownership and physical ability to walk or bike, overlooking people with disabilities, the elderly, low‑income residents, and those living beyond walkable distances. Safety, accessibility, and inclusive participation are not addressed.

early_clusters.label: Impact Assessment and Over‑statement
early_clusters.summary: Emission‑reduction estimates rely on fixed emission factors, a single‑day participation rate of 5 % and linear extrapolations to city‑, national‑ and global‑scale impacts. No uncertainty analysis, monitoring framework, or evidence of lasting habit change is offered, rendering the claimed climate impact likely overstated.

early_clusters.label: Data Privacy, Legal Compliance and Sponsor Governance
early_clusters.summary: The tracking app will collect location and travel data, yet the proposal provides no privacy policy, no compliance plan with Argentina’s Ley de Protección de Datos Personales, and no mitigation of potential conflicts of interest with private sponsors (e.g., Google).

early_clusters.label: Presentation and Documentation Quality
early_clusters.summary: Every reviewer notes typographical errors, missing figures, inconsistent tables, and vague methodological descriptions. The lack of a clear structure hampers comprehension and verification of assumptions.

early_open_questions: What concrete outreach and recruitment strategy will be used to achieve the assumed 5 % city‑wide participation, and how will its effectiveness be measured?
How will the mobile app ensure compliance with Argentine data‑protection law, including consent, data minimisation, storage, and the right to delete, especially given involvement of private sponsors?
What is the detailed budget for app development, security personnel, media campaign, prizes, and any other operational costs, and which public or private funding sources will cover them?
How will the proposal quantify and verify actual GHG emission reductions, incorporate uncertainty analysis, and guard against rebound effects (e.g., increased car use after the event)?
What specific measures will be taken to include people with disabilities, the elderly, low‑income residents, and those without smartphones, ensuring equitable participation and safety?
Beyond the one‑day event, what long‑term behaviour‑change mechanisms (e.g., infrastructure upgrades, policy incentives) are planned to sustain reduced car use and broader climate benefits?

feasibility.rationale: Organising a city‑wide walking day and a tracking app is technically possible, but the proposal hinges on uncertain government approval, substantial funding for incentives, and achieving 5 % population participation without a clear outreach plan.

novelty.rationale: Similar "car‑free" or "walk to work" campaigns exist globally; the idea of a single‑day event with gamified app tracking adds modest variation but is not a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: The quantitative emission‑saving estimates rely on simplistic assumptions (fixed emission factor, constant travel distance, 5 % uptake) and extrapolate to national/world scales without uncertainty analysis, making the claimed impact likely overstated.

presentation.rationale: The document contains numerous typographical errors, inconsistent formatting, and vague references; key methodological details (e.g., algorithm for GHG calculation, data‑privacy safeguards) are missing, reducing clarity and credibility.

A1.perspective_summary: The proposal outlines a feasible one‑day walking event supported by a mobile app, but it offers limited novelty and relies on optimistic participation and emission‑reduction assumptions that are not rigorously justified. Scientific credibility is weakened by oversimplified calculations and a lack of detailed methodology, while the presentation hampers clear evaluation.
key_concerns: Assumed 5 % city‑wide participation lacks a substantiated recruitment strategy.
Emission factors and travel distances are treated as constants, ignoring modal shift complexities and rebound effects.
Scaling of short‑term savings to national/world levels is performed without propagation of uncertainty.
No discussion of data privacy, app accuracy, or validation of the GHG‑saving algorithm.
Cost estimates are vague; no budget breakdown or funding source is identified.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires multiple regulatory approvals (public safety, data privacy, transport route adjustments) and a coordinated rollout of a new mobile app. The proposal provides no concrete plan for procurement, legal basis for personal data collection, or budget, making implementation unlikely without substantial new legislation or executive orders.

novelty.rationale: While awareness days and car‑free initiatives exist, the combination of a city‑wide walking day, prize‑based incentives, and real‑time emissions accounting via a smartphone app adds a modestly new element, but the core concept is not unprecedented.

impact.rationale: The calculated emissions savings (≈0.38 % of transport sector emissions) are marginal and rely on optimistic participation rates and behavior change that are not substantiated with a monitoring framework. Governance‑related impacts (e.g., improved data for transport planning) are possible but not clearly articulated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal mixes narrative, technical calculations, and business‑model canvases without a clear hierarchy. Critical governance details—budget, responsible agencies, risk mitigation, legal compliance—are missing, reducing readability for decision‑makers.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance standpoint, the "LET'S WALK!" event is an ambitious awareness campaign that lacks a realistic regulatory pathway, clear institutional responsibilities, and a robust accountability structure. While the idea of incentivized walking and data‑driven emissions tracking is somewhat novel, the proposal’s assumptions about participation, emissions reductions, and government support are not substantiated, raising doubts about its practical execution and measurable impact.
key_concerns: Absence of a data‑privacy framework for the mobile‑app tracking; unclear compliance with Argentine personal data law (Ley de Protección de Datos Personales).
No defined procurement process or budget for app development, security forces, health personnel, and media outreach; potential fiscal constraints.
Reliance on private sponsors (e.g., Google) for critical infrastructure without addressing conflict‑of‑interest or public‑sector procurement rules.
Unclear authority and coordination mechanism between the city government, transport agencies, and NGOs to modify routes or promote home‑office for a single day.
Equity concerns: the event assumes walking or biking is feasible for all participants, ignoring accessibility for people with disabilities or long commutes.
Over‑optimistic participation assumptions (5 % of city population) without a proven outreach or incentive strategy.
Lack of a monitoring and evaluation plan to verify claimed emissions savings and habit‑change outcomes.

feasibility.rationale: The event requires simultaneous government authorization, coordination with transport operators, sponsors, and the development of a custom mobile app. No budget, staffing plan, or detailed timeline is provided, and the target of 5 % city‑wide participation in a single day is highly optimistic. These gaps create high logistical and financial risk.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑to‑work / car‑free day campaigns already exist worldwide, and the use of a smartphone app to log steps and award prizes is a common feature in many health‑ and sustainability‑focused apps. The proposal does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism or technology.

impact.rationale: The calculated emissions savings amount to only ~0.38 % of the transportation sector’s annual emissions and depend on a one‑day behavior change. There is no evidence that the event will generate lasting habit shifts, so the long‑term climate impact is uncertain and likely modest.

presentation.rationale: The document is poorly structured, contains numerous typographical errors, references to missing figures, and calculations that lack clear sourcing. This hampers comprehension and makes it difficult to verify the claimed benefits.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational standpoint, LET'S WALK! hinges on extensive inter‑agency coordination, undefined funding, and a custom app that has not been scoped. While the concept mirrors existing car‑free days, the proposal offers little novelty and its projected climate impact is limited to a single‑day emission dip. The presentation is fragmented, leaving many implementation assumptions unsubstantiated.
key_concerns: Dependence on government approval and coordination with multiple public agencies without a clear engagement plan.
Absence of a detailed budget for app development, security, media, prizes, and staffing.
Data‑privacy and accuracy of the GHG‑savings algorithm are not addressed.
Achieving 5 % participation in a one‑day event is unrealistic without massive outreach and incentives.
Incentivising companies to allow home‑office on the event day lacks a concrete value proposition for employers.
Scalability beyond Buenos Aires is unclear; each city would need its own bespoke coordination effort.
Calculations of emissions savings are based on rough assumptions and lack transparent source data.

feasibility.rationale: The event assumes 5 % of Buenos Aires residents will voluntarily walk or bike on a single day, that they all own smartphones, and that the city can quickly mobilise security, health staff, media and prize sponsors. These logistical and equity hurdles are not addressed, making real‑world execution doubtful.

novelty.rationale: City‑wide car‑free or walk‑to‑work days already exist worldwide; the proposal adds an app‑based leaderboard but does not introduce a fundamentally new mechanism for behavior change.

impact.rationale: Projected CO₂ savings rely on optimistic participation and linear extrapolations. Even if the numbers materialise, a one‑day event yields only a fraction of a percent of transport emissions. Long‑term habit change is hinted at but not substantiated with concrete follow‑up actions.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains many typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and unclear sections (e.g., cost breakdown, data‑privacy plan). While quantitative estimates are provided, the overall narrative is hard to follow for community members.

A4.perspective_summary: From the viewpoint of Buenos Aires residents, especially low‑income and mobility‑limited groups, the "Let's Walk" day promises awareness and a fun competition but overlooks practical barriers such as smartphone access, safe walking routes, and the need for sustained support beyond a single event. The claimed climate benefits appear overstated given the modest participation likely achievable.
key_concerns: Digital divide: many vulnerable residents lack smartphones or data plans to use the tracking app.
Pedestrian safety: increased foot traffic without dedicated lanes or traffic calming could raise accident risk.
Accessibility: the plan does not address people with disabilities, the elderly, or those who must transport heavy goods.
Data privacy: collecting location and travel data without a clear governance framework may deter participation.
Over‑optimistic participation assumptions (5 % of city) and linear emission calculations.
Reliance on one‑off incentives (prizes) may not translate into lasting behavioral change.
Potential displacement of traffic to other routes or times, creating new congestion or pollution hotspots.

feasibility.rationale: The event depends on coordinated government approval, a custom smartphone app, and large‑scale participation (5% of city residents). While technically possible, the plan assumes rapid stakeholder alignment, reliable data collection, and sufficient funding for security, health staff, and prizes—conditions that are uncertain in practice.

novelty.rationale: Walk‑or‑bike‑to‑work days and car‑free events already exist worldwide. The proposal adds an app‑based competition and prize system, which is an incremental twist rather than a fundamentally new approach.

impact.rationale: Estimated emissions savings (≈0.38 % of transport sector) rely on optimistic participation and behavior‑change assumptions. Moreover, benefits accrue mainly to participants who can already walk or bike, while low‑income, disabled, or long‑commute residents see little direct gain.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains typographical errors, inconsistent tables, and unclear sections, making it difficult to extract key metrics and evaluate assumptions.

A5.perspective_summary: From an equity and justice standpoint, the proposal privileges residents with smartphones, short commutes, and physical ability, while marginalising those who rely on cars for longer trips or lack digital access. It also raises privacy concerns by handing location data to large tech firms and risks diverting public resources to a one‑day event whose climate impact is modest. The claimed long‑term habit change is not substantiated by structural transport reforms.
key_concerns: Exclusion of people without smartphones, with disabilities, or who live beyond walkable distances, reinforcing existing mobility inequities.
Potential misuse of granular location data by Google or sponsors, lacking clear privacy safeguards.
Assumption that incentives will generate lasting behavioral change without addressing systemic issues such as affordable public transit or safe cycling infrastructure.
Public costs (security, health personnel, media) may be borne by taxpayers while private sponsors reap branding benefits.
Risk of greenwashing: modest emissions reductions are presented as large‑scale impact, which could mislead stakeholders.

feasibility.rationale: Coordinating a city‑wide one‑day event, developing a tracking app, securing government authorization and sponsor prizes is logistically complex. The proposal assumes 5 % population participation without evidence, and the required inter‑agency cooperation (transport, health, security) is not substantiated, lowering practical feasibility.

novelty.rationale: Car‑free or walk‑to‑work days, app‑based distance tracking, and incentive prizes are already common in many cities. The concept does not introduce a new mechanism for systemic decarbonisation or habit formation.

impact.rationale: Estimated short‑term CO₂e savings (~66 t day⁻¹) are modest for a city of 2.9 M people, and the long‑term impact projections (e.g., 10 % rise in recycling, 1 % solar law adoption) are speculative and not causally linked to a single event. The proposal lacks a robust pathway to durable behavioural or policy change.

presentation.rationale: The document is lengthy, contains numerous typographical errors, inconsistent units, and unclear tables, making it difficult to extract key assumptions and metrics. The narrative jumps between event description, business model, and extrapolated global impacts without clear hierarchy.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long‑term sustainability standpoint, the "Let's Walk!" event is an awareness‑raising activity with limited systemic leverage. While it could generate modest short‑term emission reductions and some health co‑benefits, the proposal overstates its capacity to trigger broader policy shifts or lasting behavioural change. The feasibility hinges on uncertain participation rates and extensive coordination that are not convincingly addressed.
key_concerns: Assumption of 5 % city‑wide participation lacks empirical support; actual uptake may be far lower.
Long‑term impact claims (recycling increase, solar law, electric bus adoption) are not causally linked to a one‑day event.
